data.name: Max Crocombe
data.description: Of Cook Islands heritage, Maxime Teremoana Crocombe was born in Auckland before moving to England as a youngster. He was the man between the sticks in Salford City’s National League North winning season in 2017-18 – his form earning him a national debut for the All Whites in a friendly against Canada in 2018, the first of more than 20 caps to date. He was a key force for Grimsby when they became the first team to eliminate five teams from higher divisions in a single FA Cup season, reaching the quarter-finals in 2023. In total, he has been at 10 clubs in the UK before cracking it with Millwall in the Championship: he was first-choice keeper by Christmas 2025. Along the way there’s been plenty of time on the bench. “I’m not really one to sulk about it,” he told the BBC. “I always believe I will play.” He’s twice earned the Golden Glove award at international tournaments and has retained the trust of the All Whites' head coach, Darren Bazeley. He’s been nominated as having the best banter in the squad, leading his fellow All White Ben Waine to say: “Sometimes it’s dry humour but he is quite funny.”

data.name: Tim Payne
data.description: One of the first names to go on the All Whites starting XI, Payne is valued for his versatility. He can fill in at centre-back and in defensive midfield, although his main position is right-back. “His experience is phenomenal, he can play in a lot of positions across the backline,” one of his Phoenix coaches said. He made his All Whites debut at just 18 in 2012 and the same year played in NZ’s team at the London Olympics. His best break seemed to be signing with Blackburn but work visa problems limited his appearances at age-group level and, apart from a season with the Portland Timbers in the US, he has mainly stayed with the Wellington Phoenix, making more than 150 appearances for them in the A-League. On the eve of the tournament he became a social media star after an influencer from Argentina called on his followers to unite and make the little-known 32-year-old a “hero” of the upcoming World Cup.

data.name: Francis de Vries
data.description: De Vries is valued not only for his outstanding defensive skills but also the grounded attitude and mentoring he brings to the squad. He’s had spells with the MLS side Vancouver Whitecaps and the Swedish club Värnamo but there have been setbacks too, prompting him to read up on sports psychology. He credits developing mental skills as helping his performance on the football pitch and has set up a business to help young players in this area. “Football is a teacher of skills that will help you in all areas of your life,” he told the Unused Subs podcast. “Football has to mean something more than results – that’s where real fulfilment, real connections come.” The philosophy is paying dividends in Auckland, where fans chose him as player of the season in 2024-25, and for the All Whites, where he is a regular starter.

data.name: Tyler Bindon
data.description: Bindon is following in his former Football Ferns goalkeeper mum Jenny’s footsteps by representing NZ at a World Cup. As a child Bindon was a common fixture on the sidelines of the Ferns’ training sessions. His dad, Grant Bindon, is a former New Zealand volleyball captain and met Jenny while they were students in Illinois. Bindon came through the Major League Soccer system but opted for the All Whites despite attracting interest from the US selectors at under-19 level. It is believed Tyler and Jenny are the first mother and son duo to play football at the Olympics. The former All Whites captain Ryan Nelsen believes Bindon “ticks every box” to succeed in the Premier League (he has signed with Nottingham Forest and spent 2025-26 on loan at Sheffield United) and will go on to become New Zealand’s best ever player. “He’s got all the qualities needed, including his calmness under pressure when on the ball, he says. A goalscoring threat at set pieces, he leapt high to head home from a corner for his first goal at Sheffield United, with his parents over from NZ in the stands.

data.name: Michael Boxall
data.description: After nine years at Minnesota he is the all-time leader for starts and appearances and is a key figure in its backline. He says he is waiting for all the young guys to run past him, but “I’m still waiting”. Commentators praise his “massive physical presence” and his leadership skills; however, he does not like the off-season and the solo training it requires. “You go to some dark places and I just think of some creative ways to keep me going,” he says. Boxall, who is of Samoan heritage, was once the target of racial abuse during a match against Qatar in Austria. It led to the All Whites refusing to play the second half and they also filed a complaint with Fifa. “I’m proud of my teammates for taking the stand they did.” After 55 appearances for the All Whites without scoring, he bagged one in NZ’s 3-0 win over New Caledonia to help the side qualify for this World Cup. Fun fact: he collects sports shoes. “I’ve got a few rare Nikes and a few Jordans as well that I only bust out when it’s not raining or snowing,” he says

data.name: Joe Bell
data.description: In a squad with star names at both ends of the pitch, Bell is more than content with a role in the shadows, as he told Fifa.com. “I don’t like to be in the spotlight, so I think it’s quite nice to sit there between those who score and those who save goals and it’s an important role in the team. Obviously, playing in the centre of the pitch, there’s a lot of responsibility to be good both in attack and in defence, but especially with the ball. That’s what I enjoy the most about the game, having the ball at my feet.” Bell relaxed at home in Wānaka in Central Otago over the summer having helped his Norwegian club side Viking end a 34-year league title drought which saw fans, including a cabinet minister, invade the pitch. It got his club into Champions League qualifying as well so he could be “ticking some of the two biggest boxes you can tick” in 2026. Rated by his club for being good in individual duels and confident on the ball, Bell is aiming for a starting place come World Cup time but says “supporting and helping the team” is most important – a sensible attitude from a man whose middle name is Zen.

data.name: Logan Rogerson
data.description: Rogerson was called up on the day of the All Whites’ first game against Iran after Peterborough’s Matt Garbett was injured in training. The winger earned his first cap in 2015 aged 17 but had to make the move to Europe in order to make it professionally. He landed at Carl Zeiss Jana in Germany’s third tier but injury prevented him from breaking into senior football. “Football is a cut-throat sport,” he admitted recently. After spells in Finland and Azerbaijan he returned to New Zealand in 2024 to sign for Auckland FC, winning the A-League title in May of this year. “Matty’s a good mate of mine and I am really gutted for him,” he said after his late call-up. “It’s a weird feeling to be honest, slightly conflicted as I am also buzzing to get out there and meet up with the team.”

data.name: Marko Stamenić
data.description: One of the most important players in the All Whites mix for his physicality and technical ability with the head coach, Darren Bazeley, admiring the midfielder’s “consistency in and out of possession”. Bazeley told the NZ Herald newspaper: “He’s tactically very astute.” Stamenic wants to help the All Whites “make achievements that probably no one has thought about with the World Cup and how far we can actually go”. In a three-year spell he has lifted trophies in Denmark, Serbia and Greece. A career highlight was the chance to live in his dad’s home country, Serbia, and absorb its culture, food and celebrations. “I’ve met very few Samoan-Serbs …it felt quite special. I love embracing both cultures …” Fun fact: His mum once made 30 Ferrero Rocher chocolate leis (garlands) for the All Whites and presented them to the players after a match against Samoa.

data.name: Chris Wood
data.description: What Ronaldo is for Portugal, Wood is for the All Whites: goalscorer and endlessly loyal to his country, so fans have been fretting about his fitness in the World Cup buildup. An inspiration for youngsters, he’s “the poster boy of the All Whites, he's definitely the standout”, the Radio New Zealand journalist Mathew Nash wrote before the knee injury that derailed his latest Premier League season. Wood had an outstanding 2024-25, becoming the first Forest player in 30 years to score 20 plus goals in a season and the first Kiwi to be voted a Premier League player of the month. The youngest ever All Whites captain says: “Playing for my country makes me extremely proud and honoured.” Returned from knee surgery in April and is hopeful of staying at the top for some time: “I'm not one with blistering pace, so I'm never gonna lose it. So that's good coming into my older years where I can actually still continue to do what I did when I was 22 years old.”

data.name: Sarpreet Singh
data.description: Singh started all 10 of New Zealand’s internationals in 2025 and will be a key force in the World Cup if he recovers from a major knee injury. His signing for Bayern Munich in 2019 caused huge excitement at home but he could not break into the first team and stints with other European clubs followed. He spent the first half of 2026 back at Phoenix, where his career began, on loan from the Serbian club Backa Topola. Born in Auckland to Indian parents who migrated from Punjab, Singh will become only the second Indian-origin player at the World Cup in its almost 100-year history. “I will do my best because I know I represent a lot of people. I want to try and create a pathway for future Indian players,” he told the Times of India. “Our goal is to get out of the group stage, and I don’t think we have to change too much [to achieve that].”

data.name: Elijah Just
data.description: Just is of Chinese descent through his mother. Despite his small 1.70m (5ft 6in) frame, he is comfortable with headers and is a smart attacker who thrives in tight spaces. He is often used on the right wing or as an attacking midfielder for both club and country. He debuted for the All Whites at just 19 and says it has been rewarding to move from being a fan to wearing the strip, especially having his parents in the stand when the side qualified for the World Cup against New Caledonia in March 2025. It was “pretty special”, he told Radio New Zealand. Signing for Motherwell last year was another important step in ensuring his form would help him earn selection and game time at the World Cup, he told the Friends of Football website.

data.name: Alex Paulsen
data.description: Paulsen is a special talent, who is seeking to displace Max Crocombe for the No 1 position in the All Whites. His four-year deal with Bournemouth – “A big moment for me and my family,” he said – when he was just 21 made him one of very few Kiwis to clinch a Premier League deal. “The intensity and the difference in how they train is just so competitive,” he told the Unused Subs podcast. The Cherries, no doubt impressed by his agility and reflexes, see him as a long-term project and he went on loan first to Auckland FC, where he was pivotal in them becoming “Premiers” in their first season in the A-League, and Lechia Gdańsk. His attitude, technique and passing are second to none, his All White teammate Ben Old says. Old recalls the time his best mate was videoing his juggling skills on his new phone and a ball hit the phone which sailed out a small gap in the window of his 13th floor hotel room. “My first thought was, my mum’s gonna kill me,” Paulsen told the Unused Subs.

data.name: Liberato Cacace
data.description: Cacace is a key All White, being named as a stand-in captain during Chris Wood’s injury-enforced layoff. He is building on a career that began as a teen at the Wellington Phoenix before attracting the attention of the Belgian club Sint-Truiden. That led to four seasons with the Italian club Empoli – he was the first Kiwi to play in Serie A, much to the delight of his dad, who is from Naples and introduced his son to football when he was three years old. Cacace’s signing with Wrexham attracted huge publicity at home due to the popularity of the TV series; however, a run of injuries has made for a difficult start. Fun fact: Cacace’s dad toasted the deal with the Wrexham manager, Phil Parkinson, and other club officials when they visited his restaurant in Wellington, as the side were preparing for a friendly against the Phoenix.

data.name: Alex Rufer
data.description: Rufer is from Kiwi football royalty – his dad, Shane, is a former All White, while his uncle Wynton won four titles with Werder Bremen and was named the Oceania footballer of the century. Alex is his club’s longest serving player having made his debut as a 17-year-old and in 2024 his leadership, ball-winning ability, and distribution led to him being named in the Professional Footbellers Australia A-League men’s team of the season. Fun fact: his family are all fishing fans and the rods are usually in the back of the car, he told the Unused Subs podcast. “It actually became something that took my mind away from football and the pressures of that.”

data.name: Nando Pijnaker
data.description: Of Dutch heritage, Pijnaker has played in Sweden, Switzerland, Portugal, Denmark and Ireland, before returning to New Zealand to join new A-League club Auckland FC in 2024. His Irish experience was a standout. He joined Sligo Rovers, which he recalls as being “in the middle of nowhere and it has terrible weather all year round”. However, this was offset by “amazing” people, who welcomed Pinjaker and fellow All White Max Mata, he told WaiBOP Football. He made 77 appearances for Sligo. Fun fact: the jersey Pijnaker wore when he scored the winning goal in the 97th minute in Auckland’s match against Sydney FC is on display at Auckland Museum, representing the club’s hugely successful first season in the A-League.

data.name: Finn Surman
data.description: After a slow start Surman was outstanding in the Timbers’ 2025 season and named the fans’ player of the year. He relishes the chance to learn from the former England player Phil Neville, who is the club’s head coach. “He’s a very enthusiastic person … It gees me up a bit,” he told the Unused Subs podcast. The 1.90m (6ft 3in) defender is impressed with the league too, rating its intensity, flair and attackers’ speed as a big step up from what he was used to in the A-League. Surman left home at 15 to sharpen his football skills but any initial nerves were eased when he discovered living at an academy hostel in Wellington “was like a massive extended sleepover”.  He’s made his mark at age-group level, including captaining the All Whites at the 2023 Under-20 World Cup, but this will be eclipsed by getting some game time at “the pinnacle of football”. Fun fact: Surman is impressed that a fan with a chainsaw cuts off a piece of log to present to each of the team’s goalscorers at Portland’s matches – carving a coffee table is the plan if he manages to earn one.

data.name: Kosta Barbarouses
data.description: Of Greek heritage, Barbarouses is the second most capped All White in the squad after Chris Wood and was first called up 18 years ago. He’s the first Kiwi to score 100 goals in the men’s A-League, has been named in the A-League men’s team of the decade and has won the championship with three different clubs. His career started as a 17-year-old schoolboy with the Wellington Phoenix in their inaugural 2007–08 season and he has returned to the club three separate times, becoming a fan favourite. He told Radio New Zealand a World Cup campaign comes at the cost of missing his daughters’ milestones such as starting school but added that he hopes they will understand when they are a bit older.

data.name: Ben Waine
data.description: Waine has tasted both sides of life as a footballer: scoring on debut for the under-20s and senior All Whites but also being frustrated by non-selection at club level and a goal drought. He has tapped into the expertise of a Kiwi sports psychologist to help him “dig in”. "I'm big on the mental side of the game and I do a lot of work on that,” he told the BBC. Having the tools to cope with the hard times has been “a massive help”, he says. During his time at Plymouth Argyle, Waine briefly played under the orders of the former England and Manchester United star Wayne Rooney. Waine has a Geordie mother so he is a supporter of Newcastle and enjoys golf and going to the beach. Hit the headlines in England in March when his goal eliminated Sunderland – of all clubs – in the FA Cup fifth round.

data.name: Ben Old
data.description: In the New Zealand context, Old is a rare talent, according to the NZ Herald journalist Michael Burgess, highlighting the player’s raw pace, close control and ability to dribble past players. Old’s assets make him a lethal weapon on the counterattack and he is only the second New Zealander, after Bill Tuiloma, to play in France’s Ligue 1, where he has had to adjust to a more aggressive, less-structured style than his Kiwi club the Phoenix. Setbacks have included a serious knee injury and the side’s relegation to Ligue 2 – but peace of mind comes with meditating before matches to use some of his best moments for inspiration. Instead of the photo shoot he was expecting on joining St Étienne, an artist painted him. “It was a truly amazing experience and a memory I’ll remember for ever,” he told Channel magazine. Fun Fact: Old was a child prodigy at golf, visiting the US aged seven to compete against other young talents, and he hopes to play at the UK’s top courses as part of his desire to relieve the pressures of top-class football.

data.name: Callum McCowatt
data.description: Was voted Silkeborg's player of the 2025 season by fans as well as being the club’s top scorer in all competitions. The main difference he’s noticed being based for six years in Scandinavia? “Players are much more mentally stronger and it's evident from the moment you walk in the door, which is something that I think New Zealand players should work on – myself included. I've taken years [working on it] and it's something I'm working on actively still,” he told the website Stuff. After moving through the age-group levels for New Zealand, he got his first cap in 2019 and scored a goal on debut against Ireland. He has taken to life in Denmark, which “feels like home now”, and is an admirer of how people are treated and is on the way to becoming fluent in the language. “I think maybe I've come as a boy and I'm a man now,” he says.

data.name: Jesse Randall
data.description: Being able to play in attack and on the wing has helped propel the seven-cap rising star into the World Cup squad. His excellent form with Auckland FC in the A-League, including being their top goalscorer in the latest campaign, also helped his cause. He rates his pace as his best asset. Randall’s career is now taking off after a slow start – he was never approached by an academy in his home city, Wellington, and trials in England were “pretty ruthless”, he told the website Stuff. A spell with Charleston in the US didn’t lead to much game time and as the doubts grew, his career was only revived by a late call-up from the All Whites coach, Darren Bazeley, to join his under-23 squad for the Paris Olympics. After the World Cup Randall will be Scotland-bound to start a two-year contract with Dundee United. Fun fact: Randall was just seven when he attended the 2009 match against Bahrain that saw NZ secure their last World Cup qualification. “That was a massive moment for me, knowing that’s what I wanted to do is play football.”

data.name: Michael Woud
data.description: Woud has faced many obstacles in his career, including going 620 days without a professional match. Despite being the first signing for his newly established Auckland club he was promptly benched in favour of his fellow All White Alex Paulsen. “It was tough and there were some dark moments,” he told the Unused Subs podcast.  As a teen, Woud left NZ after signing for Sunderland but never made it further than the academy. There were spells in the Netherlands and Japan too and his luck has changed this year: he’s World Cup-bound as the third-choice keeper thanks to an injury to another contender, Oli Sail.

data.name: Ryan Thomas
data.description: Arguably the most gifted Kiwi player in a generation, he has had a career blighted by knee injuries – leading to gruesome surgery, requiring his knees to be broken and lowered. He was only 19 when he made his debut for the All Whites and also starred for PEC Zwolle as a teenager, scoring twice in their shock 5-1 defeat of Ajax in the 2014 Dutch Cup final. He moved to PSV Eindhoven but in his first week ruptured an anterior cruciate ligament. Out of the national side for six years, he went through dark times struggling with the mental impact of his injuries, which led to him not watching football any more. However, the surgery has now made him pain-free and he returned to the All Whites last year. “I'm just really happy to be part of the group again and being a part of something bigger than just club football,” he told Radio New Zealand regarding his World Cup hopes. The Dutch-Kiwi has been speaking only Dutch for five years and in an All Whites’ setting feels “a little bit of an idiot”, trying to converse in English. “I'm not exactly a Kiwi any more and it kind of annoys me," he says.

data.name: Callan Elliot
data.description: Elliot started as an attacker and morphed into a defender in part because he saw shortages at his club. He describes himself as a "modern-style" full-back who likes to get forward. After starting out at the Wellington Phoenix, he signed for the Greek side Xanthi and then Motherwell on loan but they weren’t happy times. He told the website Stuff about breaks in his career when he hasn’t got game time or didn’t have a club: “Periods like those suck, but at the end of the day, playing football is still what I want to do, so that keeps me going.” For the All Whites he’s a back-up to Tim Payne and Storm Roux. Fun fact: Scottish-born, he loves Edinburgh. “I just think it’s a beautiful place. I always say to people if you get Edinburgh on a sunny day, it’s absolutely unbelievable,” he told the BBC.

data.name: Lachlan Bayliss
data.description: Bayliss won his first cap only in March, with the coach, Darren Bazeley, saying he’s “a bit of a bolter”.  Bayliss has met Bazeley’s challenge to aim higher after he played well for New Zealand Under-23s at the 2024 Olympics. Bayliss has had an outstanding season in the A-League with the Newcastle Jets, assisting and scoring goals. “He’s an exciting player – very dangerous off the bench,” the pundit Paul Ifill said. Australian-born, he qualifies through his late father, who was a Kiwi. Playing for the All Whites would make his late father “very proud” he says.  “It would mean everything. I definitely feel a strong connection with New Zealand through him,” Bayliss told 1News.

data.name: Tommy Smith
data.description: Only Smith and Chris Wood remain from the 2010 squad that went to the World Cup in South Africa. He is now the fifth-choice centre back, but Darren Bazeley, the coach, rates his values and leadership: “Tommy has been very strong in that space.” Smith, meanwhile, told the Unused Subs podcast: “The centre-backs who are ahead of me in the pecking order [for the All Whites] are playing at a fantastic level, and my minutes are probably going to be limited, and that’s fine. I’ve come to terms with that.” It has been a distinguished career for a man once told by Roy Keane: “My nan could have run faster than you and she’s dead.” The pair crossed swords at Ipswich when Keane was an “intimidating” manager. However, it was Keane who encouraged the English-born Smith to switch his allegiance to NZ and he says he has never regretted it. The former All Whites skipper Ryan Nelsen believes it was only bad luck that stopped Smith from signing for a Premier League club. “I played alongside players who weren’t as good as Tommy,” he says. After a successful season with Auckland, Smith is playing in the English fifth tier for family reasons.
